text: What a teaser!  So what do the edges in the diagram at the end mean?  The "click" edge seems to show signals.  It appears that "add 1 to the count" is a label for the node.  The orange numbers seem to show the value of "the count."  But why the hell isn't "the count" reified as a visual object?

text: My quest to build a truly personal computer has hit a major milestone. I finally have a working reference hardware implementation for my uCISC processor at the TinyFPGA Bx board. It runs a battery of instruction tests and computes fib(24) successfully at 16MHz (4 clocks per instruction). See the readme and reference folder here: <https://github.com/grokthis/ucisc/tree/master/hardware>

text:
<@UFB8STN7K>'s expressiveness benchmark[1] reminds me:

Watching this talk helped me realize why I never really got the Felleisen paper: their notion of "expressiveness" is almost diametrically opposite what I (and, I think, most people) associate with the term.

<https://pwlconf.org/2019/shriram-krishnamurthi>

As an almost trivial example, they say that a `for` loop is not more expressive than a `while` loop, because it can be rewritten with a local transform (Lisp macro). I consider it _more_ expressive because it creates a new structure for loops that allows us to reason about updates separately from the body. Lisp macros are powerful because they allow my program to get progressively more expressive. Isn't this the normal meaning of the term? A more expressive language helps you express intent more tersely.

The paper is cool. But they need to get off my lawn and find a new term for themselves. See also <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Expressive_power_%28computer_science%29#Information_description>

[1] <https://willcrichton.net/expressiveness-benchmark>

text: This was really fun demo. We've talked a lot about the benefits of projectional editing over plain text editing, but seeing it in action is great.

elements.text: This was really fun demo. We've talked a lot about the benefits of projectional editing over plain text editing, but seeing it in action is great.

text:
This teaser has a pretty narrow viewport -- so there are some other components you're not seeing here. We think it'll be better (both for comprehension and productivity) to rely on multiple synced representations instead of trying to present everything at once through the schematic.

The count (and everything else that isn't featured in the schematic) lands in the data sheet, which brings together _everything_ about the thing you're working on into a single editable document (structure and live values included).

By being a bit more selective about what we put into the schematic view, we can ensure that it never ends up too crowded to be useful. It provides an intuitive understanding of the flows and relationships you're looking at in the data sheet -- and by interacting in the data sheet, you can tell us where the schematic's attention should be focused.

text:
> It appears that "add 1 to the count" is a label for the node.
Yep! The schematic system can provide arbitrary annotations for the nodes and wires. In this case it's showing the code of the process that runs on click. We're still experimenting with the visuals here, so that should be more clear in time.

text:
I was kidding about the lawn, hopefully that was obvious :slightly_smiling_face: I don't really have a lawn if I go toe to toe against Shriram Krishnamurthy. This thread was an effort to spread the word on what they did. Hopefully by highlighting what got in the way for me so others who share the misconception don't spend as much time not getting it.

Particularly when doing a retrospective, it feels like a missed opportunity to not point out the two possible meanings. (I disagree that Shriram means what I mean, just in a more precise way. The two interpretations here have little in common. Sometimes we need to contrast two Lisp macros and debate which is more expressive, more clearly conveying intention. This paper wouldn't help there.)

The two interpretations here have little in common. Sometimes we need to contrast two Lisp macros and debate which is more expressive, more clearly conveying intention. This paper wouldn't help there.)

text: 55:30 (<https://youtu.be/43XaZEn2aLc?t=3329>)

My early goals, at least currently, are 1) VGA 640x480 screen, 2) UART for text input, 3) nintendo era game controllers - with that I can load code over UART, send text and "type" over UART, and program some simple "games". It's a really good POC that roughly equates to the capabilities of a C64 at that point and I can switch focus to better uCISC and higher level languages and see where I can take it.

text: I took it as a tool for language designers, to be used something like: "go crazy with your features as long as they don't increase expressive power (while loops! for loops! do/while loops!), but as soon as your feature increases expressive power, be very careful in adding new stuff"

elements.text: I took it as a tool for language designers, to be used something like: "go crazy with your features as long as they don't increase expressive power (while loops! for loops! do/while loops!), but as soon as your feature increases expressive power, be very careful in adding new stuff"

text: also as an end user I always want to use the most powerful tool available, but according to this definition I could be making my life easier by finding a language with less expressive power that still is able to accomplish the given task

elements.text: also as an end user I always want to use the most powerful tool available, but according to this definition I could be making my life easier by finding a language with less expressive power that still is able to accomplish the given task

text:
Particularly if you have existing expectations.

If you're starting from scratch with a new system, you want it to be as powerful as possible. But _once you start using it_, moving the goalposts often feels hostile.

text: I'd propose to use the two terms "expressive" and "powerful"

elements.text: I'd propose to use the two terms "expressive" and "powerful"

text:
Expressive is the code giving you more information, more to reason about. E.g. for is more expressive than while.

However, while is more powerful than if, as it empowers the user: you can do more with it.

text:
hi <@UN9SCH5RD>
1. The bundling when you open the project? We actually compile all the packages you have added and then when in node you write `require('ramda')`  we actually wrap your `module.export` in function defining require that just returns module from an array
2. Yes we use monaco, we did use CodeMirror but it was a bit buggy although we had vim bindings there and guess what, all beta testers wanted VSCode bindings… We might add them back if there is need among user base
3. We actually do have experimental watch functionality but it’s not part of the main app just yet. Again people wanted this because “I’m so used in VSCode!” (really) so switching to monaco and having autocomplete for core nodes stuff mitigates that a bit

text: It's the other way around? `while` and `for` are of equal power because you can locally rewrite one into the other. The paper is really discussing power. "Local rewrites" is a finer gradation of power than the leap from context-sensitive to Turing-complete languages. But it feels like it belongs on that spectrum.

elements.text: It's the other way around?
while
and
for

are of equal power because you can locally rewrite one into the other. The paper is really discussing power. "Local rewrites" is a finer gradation of power than the leap from context-sensitive to Turing-complete languages. But it feels like it belongs on that spectrum.

text:
<https://github.com/rasendubi/meta|Meta> is the project I've been working on for the last 5 months. It's kind of a catch-all for my experiments on projectional editors, extensible environments, programming languages, and whatnot.

I've been through two rewrites. The last rewrite re-implements everything from scratch in Rust, including database, editor, compiler, vm, and even a GUI framework.

I have no impressive results to show (yet), but here is my journey so far: <https://www.alexeyshmalko.com/2020/meta-1/>

I'm mostly lurking here, so sorry for shameless advertising - but in case this was of interest to someone - my university (University of Kent, UK), which is close enough to London (where I actually live) is looking for a Lecturer (Assistant Professor). We have a few weirdos like myself and Stephen Kell, so it's not a bad place for Future of Coding. We also have a number of PhD positions - so if you wanted to work with me or anyone from the school on Future of Coding for a PhD, let me know!

<https://jobs.kent.ac.uk/vacancy.aspx?ref=CEMS-003-20>

Really love the visuals. Is this all browser based or some custom rendering?

While definitely different in flavor. It does remind me a bit of the eve prototype you talked about raising on. Where you had basically executable specifications (link at bottom). I'm curious what you did differently here that made you like this more.

It seems from the description there is no datalog going on. So maybe getting rid of the unification like semantics was the key? Definitely excited to see more examples and play with it in the future.

<https://youtu.be/5V1ynVyud4M?t=803>
